name: Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Revelation Phrase
description: Also known as Babylon's Fall: Phrase-by-Phrase Parallels.
summary: The sacred scriptures and writings that form the foundation of Christian faith and theology.
structure: Comparative Analysis - Phrase-by-phrase parallels across three prophetic texts
influence: Demonstrates continuity in prophetic tradition; influenced messianic interpretation

author: Multiple - Isaiah, Jeremiah, John (Apostle)

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Babylon (Isaiah)
keyMyths.description: Isaiah prophesies the destruction of Babylon, a symbol of worldly power and oppression, due to its wickedness and pride. The prophecy details its sudden and complete ruin, emphasizing divine judgment.

keyMyths.title: Jeremiah's Prophecies Against Babylon
keyMyths.description: Jeremiah delivers extensive oracles against Babylon, foretelling its downfall at the hands of various nations. He highlights Babylon's idolatry and its role as an instrument of God's wrath against Israel, ultimately destined for desolation.

keyMyths.title: The Destruction of Babylon (Revelation)
keyMyths.description: Revelation depicts the symbolic destruction of "Babylon the Great," representing a corrupt and persecuting world system. The imagery includes plagues, fire, and lamentation from those who profited from its luxury, signifying the ultimate triumph of good over evil.

keyMyths.title: The Exodus
keyMyths.description: The Exodus narrative, where the Israelites are freed from slavery in Egypt, serves as a foundational myth of liberation and divine intervention. It establishes a pattern of God rescuing his people from oppression, echoed in the fall of Babylon.

keyMyths.title: The Day of the Lord
keyMyths.description: The Day of the Lord is a recurring theme in prophetic literature, representing a time of divine judgment and cosmic upheaval. The destruction of Babylon in Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Revelation is often associated with this cataclysmic event.

sources.name: The Holy Bible (King James Version)
sources.description: A widely used translation of the Christian scriptures, including the Old Testament (containing Isaiah and Jeremiah) and the New Testament (containing Revelation).
sources.title: The Holy Bible (King James Version)

sources.name: Brown-Driver-Briggs Hebrew and English Lexicon
sources.description: A standard lexicon for understanding the original Hebrew texts of the Old Testament.
sources.title: Brown-Driver-Briggs Hebrew and English Lexicon

sources.name: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature (BDAG)
sources.description: A comprehensive lexicon for interpreting the Greek text of the New Testament.
sources.title: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature (BDAG)

sources.description: A classic theological commentary providing insights into the historical and spiritual context of Isaiah's prophecies.
sources.title: Commentary on Isaiah by John Calvin

sources.description: A scholarly commentary on the Book of Revelation, exploring its symbolism and theological significance.
